perception.who_is_this_for_a: Individual used-car buyers who want to buy one official Carfax report quickly, with a clearly visible secondary path for dealers needing volume access.
perception.who_is_this_for_b: Individual buyers and dealers who are skeptical and want reassurance from a visible business identity, phone support, named account managers, and human contact before buying or setting up access.

reasoning: Candidate A better satisfies the hybrid conversion brief as rendered. Its first viewport immediately shows the official Carfax offer, $9.99 price, savings cue, VIN purchase path, dealer access path, sample report link, and adjacent safety proof. The dealer card is concise but materially visible, and the full page includes real-looking dealer tiers, pricing, terms, sample report proof, legitimacy explanation, refund language, and a repeated purchase CTA. Candidate B has stronger human-contact and business-identity signals in the hero, so trust is more visibly foregrounded, but it feels less finished and less operationally specific: many visible placeholders appear in legal identity, AM names, phone numbers, photos, testimonials, and dealer pricing. Its mobile viewport is also more cluttered, with awkward navigation wrapping and less efficient use of the first screen. Overall, A is clearer, faster, more balanced between individual and dealer paths, and more usable on mobile.
blocking_visual_issue_a: Visible placeholder brand and registration details weaken perceived legitimacy, but the core offer and paths remain clear.
blocking_visual_issue_b: Many visible placeholders in business identity, people, contact details, testimonials, and dealer pricing make the trust-forward concept feel unfinished and less credible; mobile navigation also wraps awkwardly.
